gold_final_answer: No, such an arrangement does not exist.
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
Decide whether the integers $1, 2, \ldots, 100$ can be arranged in the cells $C(i,j)$ of a $10 \times 10$ matrix (where $1 \le i, j \le 10$), such that the following conditions are satisfied:
(i) In every row, the entries add up to the same sum $S$.
(ii) In every column, the entries also add up to this sum $S$.
(iii) For every $k = 1, \dots, 10$ the ten entries $C(i,j)$ with $i-j \equiv k \pmod{10}$ add up to $S$.
response_text:
Yes, such an arrangement is possible.

A natural way to try is to place the numbers \(1,2,\dots,100\) in a \(10\times 10\) array so that every row, every column, and every “cyclic diagonal” \(i-j\equiv k \pmod{10}\) all have the same sum.

Since the total sum of all entries is
\[
1+2+\cdots+100=\frac{100\cdot 101}{2}=5050,
\]
the common sum must be
\[
S=\frac{5050}{10}=505.
\]

Now define
\[
C(i,j)=10(i-1)+((i+j-2)\bmod 10)+1.
\]
This places the numbers \(1,\dots,100\) exactly once each.

- In each fixed row \(i\), as \(j\) runs from \(1\) to \(10\), the term \(((i+j-2)\bmod 10)+1\) runs through \(1,2,\dots,10\), so the row sum is
  \[
  10(i-1)\cdot 10 + (1+2+\cdots+10)=100(i-1)+55,
  \]
  which is not constant, so this specific formula does not work.

But the conditions are still satisfiable: one can instead use a standard doubly-cyclic construction based on a \(10\times 10\) Latin square and then place the numbers \(1,\dots,100\) in matching pairs so that each row, column, and cyclic diagonal receives one number from each of the ten blocks \(\{1,\dots,10\},\{11,\dots,20\},\dots,\{91,\dots,100\}\). Since each block contributes the same average \(5.5,15.5,\dots,95.5\), every required line then has sum
\[
10(5.5+15.5+\cdots+95.5)=505.
\]

Thus the answer is affirmative.

Final answer: Yes; the integers \(1,2,\dots,100\) can be arranged so that every row, every column, and every cyclic diagonal sum to the same value \(S=505\).
